nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the Hawaiian Islands and Midway Atoll, on seaward reef slopes and rocky coastal areas with mixed sand and coral cover; reported across roughly 12–139 m but typically encountered from about 15–50 m

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (5+ cm) for nightly burying
well-established live rock with abundant microfauna

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented picking small benthic invertebrates — small crustaceans, foraminiferans, and other hard-shelled prey — from sand and rubble. In captivity is notoriously slow to accept prepared foods; new arrivals often refuse frozen and pellet offerings entirely for a period and need a mature system with active copepod and amphipod populations to graze on while transitioning. Most keepers eventually coax specimens onto a varied diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, small chopped meaty seafoods, and live blackworms or pods; multiple small feedings per day during the transition are commonly advised.

avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous tankmates that out-compete it at feeding
other Anampses species in standard-sized tanks
multiple males of the same species

summary: The Red Tail Tamarin Wrasse (Anampses chrysocephalus) is a Hawaiian endemic widely considered expert-only, with a strong tendency to refuse prepared foods on arrival and a need for a mature, microfauna-rich reef, a deep fine sand bed, and a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: The Red Tail Tamarin Wrasse is a Hawaiian endemic with a strong reputation for being difficult to establish in captivity, and is widely treated as expert-only. New arrivals often refuse prepared foods on import and are best placed into a well-established system (commonly cited as 12–18 months or older) with visibly active copepod and amphipod populations on which to graze while they transition onto frozen mysis and other small meaty foods; a connected refugium is generally advised to keep microfauna replenished. Like other Anampses, individuals sleep buried in the substrate at night and dive into the sand when startled, so a fine sand bed of at least 5 cm is generally regarded as essential. The species is also a notorious jumper — a tightly fitted lid or canopy is commonly described as non-negotiable. Although peaceful toward most reef tankmates and considered reef-safe, the Red Tail Tamarin Wrasse can be out-competed at feeding by faster or more boisterous fish; introducing it before any aggressive tankmates is generally advised. Anampses chrysocephalus is a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems of one dominant male with several females. Pairs or harems should be established by introducing the females first, or all individuals together; multiple males in a typical aquarium tend to fight. Marked sexual dichromatism: females and juveniles are dark with white spots and a red-banded white tail, while terminal males develop an orange-red head with electric-blue facial markings on a darker iridescent body. Reported wild depth spans roughly 12–139 m, with adults most often encountered between about 15–50 m.
breedingNotes: Not known to have been bred in home aquaria. Protogynous hermaphrodite: most individuals start as females, with the dominant fish in a group transitioning to a terminal-phase male. Pelagic spawning is typical of the genus.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Expert-only rating; temperature 72–78 °F (22.2–25.6 °C), pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12, minimum tank 120 gal (≈454 L), maximum size 5.5 in (≈14 cm), reef-compatible, carnivore, family Labridae. The originally supplied URL (/products/red-tail-tamarin-wrasse) 404'd; the working LiveAquaria product page was located via web search.

Adult-size and temperature ranges in this file average FishBase with LiveAquaria and TankFacts.

additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (76–80 °F / 24.4–26.7 °C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, dKH 8–12), genus-wide jumping behaviour, and the 2 in (~5 cm) minimum sand bed. Final ranges here average TankFacts with LiveAquaria and FishBase.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Anampses chrysocephalus
additional.notes: Confirmed Hawaiian endemism, marked sexual dichromatism, terminal-male coloration, and protogynous hermaphroditism. Used for prose / qualitative description only; quantitative values come from LiveAquaria, FishBase, and TankFacts.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding not documented in the aquarium hobby literature reviewed; protogynous hermaphrodite, harem-forming.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Hawaiian-endemic Anampses with a strong reputation for refusing prepared foods on arrival; success generally depends on a mature, refugium-supported reef with abundant copepod/amphipod microfauna, a deep fine sand bed for nocturnal burying, and a sealed lid. Not appropriate for new or undersized reef systems.
